message:
feat(storage): add read-only fast-open path for pure-read commands

Introduce SqliteStorage::open_current_read_only(), a lightweight
opener that skips the full repair + metadata-seeding dance used by
SqliteStorage::open() when the on-disk schema header already matches
CURRENT_SCHEMA_VERSION and the runtime is compatible. It opens the
database with SQLITE_OPEN_READ_ONLY via fsqlite's open_with_flags(),
avoiding any writes — including the default-metadata reseed —
against databases that are intended to be used purely for reads in
this invocation. If the header is stale, missing, or the runtime
schema check fails, the helper returns Ok(None) so callers transparently
fall back to the full repair-capable open path.

Wire it through the CLI: a new CliOverrides.read_only_fast_open flag
is set in build_cli_overrides() only when BOTH --no-auto-import and
--no-auto-flush are present, --no-db is NOT set, and the subcommand
is one of a safe allow-list of pure readers (list, show, search,
ready, blocked, count, stale, stats, status). A new helper
supports_read_only_fast_open() owns the allow-list so mutating
commands can never accidentally opt in. open_storage_with_startup_config()
now consults the flag before falling back to
open_sqlite_storage_with_recovery().

Two storage tests pin the invariants: a current-schema DB with a
deleted metadata key must NOT be reseeded when opened via the fast
path, and a DB whose user_version has been zeroed must be refused
(returning None) so the repair path still runs. A CLI test verifies
that the flag requires both suppression flags and refuses mutating
subcommands like `create`.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
